content.text:
Families have always been at the heart of every community. But the way families live has changed a lot over time!

👨‍👩‍👧‍👦 Long ago, families were often very large. Many relatives — grandparents, aunts, uncles, and cousins — lived close together or even in the same house. This was called an extended family.

🏡 Families long ago:
• Grew or caught their own food
• Made their own clothes by hand
• Had no electricity, so they used candles for light
• Washed clothes by hand in a river or with a washboard
• Children helped with chores from a very young age

💡 Families today:
• Buy food from grocery stores
• Buy clothes from shops or online
• Use electricity for lights, refrigerators, and washing machines
• Watch television and use the internet
• Children go to school to learn reading, writing, and math

Even though daily life looks different, families then and now share the same important things: love, caring for each other, sharing meals, and telling stories.

Family traditions — special things families do together — are another way the past stays alive today.

diagrams.alt: Illustration comparing a family from the past with a family today

readingPassage.title: Layla's Great-Great-Grandmother
readingPassage.text:
Layla loved visiting her grandmother, Nana Rose. One afternoon, Nana Rose pulled out a wooden box filled with old photographs.

"Who is this?" Layla asked, pointing to a woman in a long dress standing in a field.

"That is your great-great-grandmother, Amara," said Nana Rose. "She lived over 100 years ago on a farm."

In the photo, Amara stood next to a large garden. Behind her was a small wooden house with a well nearby.

"She had ten children," Nana Rose said. "And they all worked together every day — planting vegetables, feeding animals, and cooking meals over a fire."

Layla tried to imagine her own family doing all that work. "Did they have a refrigerator?" she asked.

"No refrigerators, no electricity, no running water," Nana Rose said with a smile. "But they had something very important — they always ate dinner together and helped each other."

Layla looked at the photo for a long time. Then she said, "We still do that. We eat dinner together every night."

Nana Rose nodded. "Yes, we do. Some things never change."

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: How many children did Amara have?
comprehensionQuestions.hint: Read the paragraph where Nana Rose describes Amara's family.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What did Amara's family NOT have?
comprehensionQuestions.hint: Nana Rose lists three things they did not have — find them in the passage.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What does Layla's family still do that is the same as Amara's family?
comprehensionQuestions.hint: Look at what Layla says near the end of the passage.

comprehensionQuestions.prompt: What was in the wooden box that Nana Rose showed Layla?
comprehensionQuestions.hint: Read the very first paragraph of the story carefully.

options.text: A family with only two people
A family that moved to a new city

questions.prompt: Long ago, how did families get their food?

options.text: By growing or catching it themselves
A special thing families do together that connects them to the past
A type of food families eat
A job that only family members can do

questions.prompt: Which of these did families long ago NOT have?
What important thing do both past and present families share?

options.text: Love and caring for each other
